speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: We will be satisfied! Let us be satisfied!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Then follow me and give me audience, friends.—

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Cassius, go you into the other street

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And part the numbers.—

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Those that will hear me speak, let ’em stay here;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Those that will follow Cassius, go with him;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And public reasons shall be renderèd

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Of Caesar’s death.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: I will hear Brutus speak.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: I will hear Cassius, and compare their reasons

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: When severally we hear them renderèd.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: The noble Brutus is ascended. Silence.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Be patient till the last.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Romans, countrymen, and lovers, hear me for my

speaker: BRUTUS
text: cause, and be silent that you may hear. Believe me

speaker: BRUTUS
text: for mine honor, and have respect to mine honor

speaker: BRUTUS
text: that you may believe. Censure me in your wisdom,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: and awake your senses that you may the better

speaker: BRUTUS
text: judge. If there be any in this assembly, any dear

speaker: BRUTUS
text: friend of Caesar’s, to him I say that Brutus’ love

speaker: BRUTUS
text: to Caesar was no less than his. If then that friend

speaker: BRUTUS
text: demand why Brutus rose against Caesar, this is my

speaker: BRUTUS
text: answer: not that I loved Caesar less, but that I loved

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Rome more. Had you rather Caesar were living, and

speaker: BRUTUS
text: die all slaves, than that Caesar were dead, to live all

speaker: BRUTUS
text: freemen? As Caesar loved me, I weep for him. As he

speaker: BRUTUS
text: was fortunate, I rejoice at it. As he was valiant, I

speaker: BRUTUS
text: honor him. But, as he was ambitious, I slew him.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: There is tears for his love, joy for his fortune, honor

speaker: BRUTUS
text: for his valor, and death for his ambition. Who is

speaker: BRUTUS
text: here so base that would be a bondman? If any,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: speak, for him have I offended. Who is here so rude

speaker: BRUTUS
text: that would not be a Roman? If any, speak, for him

speaker: BRUTUS
text: have I offended. Who is here so vile that will not

speaker: BRUTUS
text: love his country? If any, speak, for him have I

speaker: BRUTUS
text: offended. I pause for a reply.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: None, Brutus, none.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Then none have I offended. I have done no

speaker: BRUTUS
text: more to Caesar than you shall do to Brutus. The

speaker: BRUTUS
text: question of his death is enrolled in the Capitol, his

speaker: BRUTUS
text: glory not extenuated wherein he was worthy, nor

speaker: BRUTUS
text: his offenses enforced for which he suffered death.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Here comes his body, mourned by Mark Antony,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: who, though he had no hand in his death, shall

speaker: BRUTUS
text: receive the benefit of his dying—a place in the

speaker: BRUTUS
text: commonwealth—as which of you shall not? With

speaker: BRUTUS
text: this I depart: that, as I slew my best lover for the

speaker: BRUTUS
text: good of Rome, I have the same dagger for myself

speaker: BRUTUS
text: when it shall please my country to need my death.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Live, Brutus, live, live!

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Bring him with triumph home unto his house.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Give him a statue with his ancestors.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Let him be Caesar.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Caesar’s better parts

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Shall be crowned in Brutus.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We’ll bring him to his house with shouts and clamors.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: My countrymen—

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Peace, silence! Brutus speaks.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Peace, ho!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Good countrymen, let me depart alone,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And, for my sake, stay here with Antony.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Do grace to Caesar’s corpse, and grace his speech

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Tending to Caesar’s glories, which Mark Antony

speaker: BRUTUS
text: (By our permission) is allowed to make.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I do entreat you, not a man depart,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Save I alone, till Antony have spoke.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Stay, ho, and let us hear Mark Antony!

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Let him go up into the public chair.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: We’ll hear him.—Noble Antony, go up.

speaker: ANTONY
text: For Brutus’ sake, I am beholding to you.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: What does he say of Brutus?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: He says for Brutus’ sake

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: He finds himself beholding to us all.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: ’Twere best he speak no harm of Brutus here.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: This Caesar was a tyrant.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Nay, that’s certain.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We are blest that Rome is rid of him.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Peace, let us hear what Antony can say.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You gentle Romans—

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Peace, ho! Let us hear him.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him.

speaker: ANTONY
text: The evil that men do lives after them;

speaker: ANTONY
text: The good is oft interrèd with their bones.

speaker: ANTONY
text: So let it be with Caesar. The noble Brutus

speaker: ANTONY
text: Hath told you Caesar was ambitious.

speaker: ANTONY
text: If it were so, it was a grievous fault,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And grievously hath Caesar answered it.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Here, under leave of Brutus and the rest

speaker: ANTONY
text: (For Brutus is an honorable man;

speaker: ANTONY
text: So are they all, all honorable men),

speaker: ANTONY
text: Come I to speak in Caesar’s funeral.

speaker: ANTONY
text: He was my friend, faithful and just to me,

speaker: ANTONY
text: But Brutus says he was ambitious,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And Brutus is an honorable man.

speaker: ANTONY
text: He hath brought many captives home to Rome,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Whose ransoms did the general coffers fill.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Did this in Caesar seem ambitious?

speaker: ANTONY
text: When that the poor have cried, Caesar hath wept;

speaker: ANTONY
text: Ambition should be made of sterner stuff.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Yet Brutus says he was ambitious,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And Brutus is an honorable man.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You all did see that on the Lupercal

speaker: ANTONY
text: I thrice presented him a kingly crown,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Which he did thrice refuse. Was this ambition?

speaker: ANTONY
text: Yet Brutus says he was ambitious,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And sure he is an honorable man.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I speak not to disprove what Brutus spoke,

speaker: ANTONY
text: But here I am to speak what I do know.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You all did love him once, not without cause.

speaker: ANTONY
text: What cause withholds you, then, to mourn for him?—

speaker: ANTONY
text: O judgment, thou art fled to brutish beasts,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And men have lost their reason!—Bear with me;

speaker: ANTONY
text: My heart is in the coffin there with Caesar,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And I must pause till it come back to me.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Methinks there is much reason in his sayings.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: If thou consider rightly of the matter,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Caesar has had great wrong.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Has he, masters?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: I fear there will a worse come in his place.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Marked you his words? He would not take the crown;

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Therefore ’tis certain he was not ambitious.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: If it be found so, some will dear abide it.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Poor soul, his eyes are red as fire with weeping.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: There’s not a nobler man in Rome than Antony.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Now mark him. He begins again to speak.

speaker: ANTONY
text: But yesterday the word of Caesar might

speaker: ANTONY
text: Have stood against the world. Now lies he there,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And none so poor to do him reverence.

speaker: ANTONY
text: O masters, if I were disposed to stir

speaker: ANTONY
text: Your hearts and minds to mutiny and rage,

speaker: ANTONY
text: I should do Brutus wrong and Cassius wrong,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Who, you all know, are honorable men.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I will not do them wrong. I rather choose

speaker: ANTONY
text: To wrong the dead, to wrong myself and you,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Than I will wrong such honorable men.

speaker: ANTONY
text: But here’s a parchment with the seal of Caesar.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I found it in his closet. ’Tis his will.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Let but the commons hear this testament,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Which, pardon me, I do not mean to read,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And they would go and kiss dead Caesar’s wounds

speaker: ANTONY
text: And dip their napkins in his sacred blood—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Yea, beg a hair of him for memory

speaker: ANTONY
text: And, dying, mention it within their wills,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Bequeathing it as a rich legacy

speaker: ANTONY
text: Unto their issue.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We’ll hear the will. Read it, Mark Antony.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: The will, the will! We will hear Caesar’s will.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Have patience, gentle friends. I must not read it.

speaker: ANTONY
text: It is not meet you know how Caesar loved you.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You are not wood, you are not stones, but men.

speaker: ANTONY
text: And, being men, hearing the will of Caesar,

speaker: ANTONY
text: It will inflame you; it will make you mad.

speaker: ANTONY
text: ’Tis good you know not that you are his heirs,

speaker: ANTONY
text: For if you should, O, what would come of it?

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Read the will! We’ll hear it, Antony.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: You shall read us the will, Caesar’s will.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Will you be patient? Will you stay awhile?

speaker: ANTONY
text: I have o’ershot myself to tell you of it.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I fear I wrong the honorable men

speaker: ANTONY
text: Whose daggers have stabbed Caesar. I do fear it.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: They were traitors. Honorable men?

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: The will! The testament!

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: They were villains, murderers. The

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: will! Read the will.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You will compel me, then, to read the will?

speaker: ANTONY
text: Then make a ring about the corpse of Caesar,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And let me show you him that made the will.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Shall I descend? And will you give me leave?

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Come down.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Descend.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: You shall have leave.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: A ring; stand round.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Stand from the hearse. Stand from the body.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Room for Antony, most noble Antony.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Nay, press not so upon me. Stand far off.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Stand back! Room! Bear back!

speaker: ANTONY
text: If you have tears, prepare to shed them now.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You all do know this mantle. I remember

speaker: ANTONY
text: The first time ever Caesar put it on.

speaker: ANTONY
text: ’Twas on a summer’s evening in his tent,

speaker: ANTONY
text: That day he overcame the Nervii.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Look, in this place ran Cassius’ dagger through.

speaker: ANTONY
text: See what a rent the envious Casca made.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Through this the well-belovèd Brutus stabbed,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And, as he plucked his cursèd steel away,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Mark how the blood of Caesar followed it,

speaker: ANTONY
text: As rushing out of doors to be resolved

speaker: ANTONY
text: If Brutus so unkindly knocked or no;

speaker: ANTONY
text: For Brutus, as you know, was Caesar’s angel.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Judge, O you gods, how dearly Caesar loved him!

speaker: ANTONY
text: This was the most unkindest cut of all.

speaker: ANTONY
text: For when the noble Caesar saw him stab,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Ingratitude, more strong than traitors’ arms,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Quite vanquished him. Then burst his mighty heart,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And, in his mantle muffling up his face,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Even at the base of Pompey’s statue

speaker: ANTONY
text: (Which all the while ran blood) great Caesar fell.

speaker: ANTONY
text: O, what a fall was there, my countrymen!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Then I and you and all of us fell down,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Whilst bloody treason flourished over us.

speaker: ANTONY
text: O, now you weep, and I perceive you feel

speaker: ANTONY
text: The dint of pity. These are gracious drops.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Kind souls, what, weep you when you but behold

speaker: ANTONY
text: Our Caesar’s vesture wounded? Look you here,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Here is himself, marred as you see with traitors.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: O piteous spectacle!

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: O noble Caesar!

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: O woeful day!

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: O traitors, villains!

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: O most bloody sight!

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We will be revenged.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Revenge! About! Seek! Burn! Fire! Kill!

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Slay! Let not a traitor live!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Stay, countrymen.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Peace there! Hear the noble Antony.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We’ll hear him, we’ll follow him,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: we’ll die with him.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Good friends, sweet friends, let me not stir you up

speaker: ANTONY
text: To such a sudden flood of mutiny.

speaker: ANTONY
text: They that have done this deed are honorable.

speaker: ANTONY
text: What private griefs they have, alas, I know not,

speaker: ANTONY
text: That made them do it. They are wise and honorable

speaker: ANTONY
text: And will no doubt with reasons answer you.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I come not, friends, to steal away your hearts.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I am no orator, as Brutus is,

speaker: ANTONY
text: But, as you know me all, a plain blunt man

speaker: ANTONY
text: That love my friend, and that they know full well

speaker: ANTONY
text: That gave me public leave to speak of him.

speaker: ANTONY
text: For I have neither wit, nor words, nor worth,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Action, nor utterance, nor the power of speech

speaker: ANTONY
text: To stir men’s blood. I only speak right on.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I tell you that which you yourselves do know,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Show you sweet Caesar’s wounds, poor poor dumb mouths,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And bid them speak for me. But were I Brutus,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And Brutus Antony, there were an Antony

speaker: ANTONY
text: Would ruffle up your spirits and put a tongue

speaker: ANTONY
text: In every wound of Caesar that should move

speaker: ANTONY
text: The stones of Rome to rise and mutiny.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: We’ll mutiny.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We’ll burn the house of Brutus.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Away then. Come, seek the conspirators.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Yet hear me, countrymen; yet hear me speak.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Peace, ho! Hear Antony, most noble Antony!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Why, friends, you go to do you know not what.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Wherein hath Caesar thus deserved your loves?

speaker: ANTONY
text: Alas, you know not. I must tell you then.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You have forgot the will I told you of.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Most true. The will! Let’s stay and hear the will.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Here is the will, and under Caesar’s seal:

speaker: ANTONY
text: To every Roman citizen he gives,

speaker: ANTONY
text: To every several man, seventy-five drachmas.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Most noble Caesar! We’ll revenge his death.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: O royal Caesar!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Hear me with patience.

speaker: PLEBEIANS
text: Peace, ho!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Moreover, he hath left you all his walks,

speaker: ANTONY
text: His private arbors, and new-planted orchards,

speaker: ANTONY
text: On this side Tiber. He hath left them you,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And to your heirs forever—common pleasures

speaker: ANTONY
text: To walk abroad and recreate yourselves.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Here was a Caesar! When comes such another?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Never, never!—Come, away, away!

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: We’ll burn his body in the holy place

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: And with the brands fire the traitors’ houses.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Take up the body.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Go fetch fire.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Pluck down benches.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: Pluck down forms, windows,

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              PLEBEIAN
text: anything.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Now let it work. Mischief, thou art afoot;

speaker: ANTONY
text: Take thou what course thou wilt.

speaker: ANTONY
text: How now, fellow?

speaker: SERVANT
text: Sir, Octavius is already come to Rome.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Where is he?

speaker: SERVANT
text: He and Lepidus are at Caesar’s house.

speaker: ANTONY
text: And thither will I straight to visit him.

speaker: ANTONY
text: He comes upon a wish. Fortune is merry

speaker: ANTONY
text: And in this mood will give us anything.

speaker: SERVANT
text: I heard him say Brutus and Cassius

speaker: SERVANT
text: Are rid like madmen through the gates of Rome.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Belike they had some notice of the people

speaker: ANTONY
text: How I had moved them. Bring me to Octavius.
